data.title: Wells Fargo Earnings Call Signals Confident Growth Path
data.description:
Wells Fargo & Company ((WFC)) has held its Q1 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 30% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

Wells Fargo’s latest earnings call carried a notably upbeat tone, as management highlighted double‑digit EPS growth, broad revenue gains, and renewed balance‑sheet momentum. While near‑term margin compression and rising expenses weighed on parts of the discussion, executives stressed strong execution, cleaner regulatory footing, and confidence in meeting their 2026 financial targets.

Strong Earnings and Revenue Growth

Diluted EPS climbed 15% from a year ago, underscoring solid profitability despite rate and cost headwinds. Revenue rose 6% year‑over‑year, powered by a 5% increase in net interest income and an 8% gain in noninterest income, showing that both lending spreads and fee businesses contributed to the top line.

Balance Sheet Growth

Loans at period‑end grew 11% year‑over‑year, topping $1.0 trillion for the first time since 2020 and signaling renewed credit demand across the franchise. Deposits increased 7% over the same period, giving the bank additional funding to support loan growth, even as the mix continues to shift toward higher‑cost balances.

Broad-Based Segment Momentum

Performance was broad‑based, with every major operating unit posting revenue gains compared with last year, a key signal of franchise health. Consumer Banking & Lending and Commercial Banking each grew revenue 7%, while CIB banking revenue rose 11%, Markets surged 19%, and Wealth & Investment Management advanced 14%.

Pre-Tax, Pre-Provision Profit Expansion

Pre‑tax, pre‑provision profit increased 14% year‑over‑year, as revenue growth outpaced rising costs from investments and compensation. This metric, closely watched by investors as a core profitability gauge, shows the underlying earnings engine remains strong even before considering credit provisions.

Strong Consumer Product Traction

Consumer activity accelerated, with new credit card accounts nearly 60% higher than a year earlier and card revenue up 5%, indicating deeper engagement and spending. Checking account openings grew 15%, and mobile active users surpassed 33 million, highlighting successful digital initiatives and growing primary banking relationships.

Auto Financing Acceleration

Auto lending saw a sharp rebound, with originations more than doubling from last year and auto revenue up 24%, pointing to regained momentum in a previously soft category. Management credited preferred relationships with Volkswagen and Audi for driving higher volumes and supporting better mix and returns.

Wealth and Asset Growth

Wealth & Investment Management client assets climbed 11% to $2.2 trillion, supported by market appreciation and strong inflows from clients. Company‑wide net asset flows reached their highest level in more than a decade, underscoring improved competitiveness and cross‑sell into investment products.

Capital Return and Capital Position

Wells Fargo returned $5.4 billion to shareholders in the quarter, including $4.0 billion of common share buybacks, reflecting confidence in capital strength and earnings power. The CET1 ratio stood at 10.3%, within the bank’s 10.0%–10.5% target range, and common shares outstanding were about 6% lower than a year ago.

Regulatory Progress

The bank closed its final outstanding consent order in March, marking the termination of 14 orders since 2019 and a major milestone in its multi‑year remediation effort. Management emphasized that this cleaner regulatory backdrop should allow greater focus on accelerating growth, improving returns, and deploying capital more flexibly.

Constructive Regulatory Rule Outlook

Early analysis of proposed capital rules suggests Wells Fargo’s risk‑weighted assets could fall by about 7%, which would modestly ease capital requirements under the current framework. The global systemic surcharge is expected to remain around 1.5%, leading management to describe the evolving regulatory environment as constructive for future capital planning.

Margin Compression

Net interest margin compressed roughly 13 basis points from the prior quarter and about 20 basis points from a year earlier, weighed down by rate cuts and mix shifts. Net interest income fell $235 million, or 2%, versus the fourth quarter, as fewer calendar days, growth in lower‑return Markets assets, and higher interest‑bearing deposit balances took a toll.

Expense Pressures and Investments

Noninterest expense rose around 3% year‑over‑year, as the bank stepped up spending on technology, including AI, as well as marketing and revenue‑linked compensation. While these investments are expected to support growth and efficiency over time, they add near‑term pressure even as management reiterates a 2026 expense target near $55.7 billion.

Home Lending Headwinds

Home lending remained a weak spot, with revenue down 9% year‑over‑year amid a strategic pullback and softer mortgage activity. Loans serviced for others fell 18%, reflecting the ongoing reduction of the third‑party servicing portfolio and declining servicing balances, which weigh on fee income.

CRE Revenue Decline

Commercial Real Estate revenue declined 21% from a year earlier, though the drop largely reflects a tough comparison rather than a sudden deterioration in fundamentals. Last year’s period included a gain from the sale of the commercial mortgage servicing business, creating a high bar for current‑year CRE revenue performance.

Isolated Credit Loss Event

Credit quality was impacted by a single fraud‑related loss in the financials‑except‑banks portfolio, specifically in real estate finance, which drove higher commercial and industrial charge‑offs. Commercial net loan charge‑offs rose modestly to 24 basis points of average loans, but management framed the event as isolated rather than indicative of broader stress.

Consumer Credit Trends and Seasonality

Consumer credit metrics reflected normal seasonal patterns, with net loan charge‑offs rising quarter‑over‑quarter to 78 basis points of average loans. Importantly, this rate was still 8 basis points lower than a year ago, and management cited seasonal credit card losses as the main driver rather than a broad deterioration in household credit.

Large NDFI Exposure Requires Monitoring

Loans to nonbank financial institutions total about $210 billion, or roughly 21% of total loans, making this a key portfolio to watch for investors. While executives emphasized diversification, strong collateral, and active risk management, they acknowledged heightened public scrutiny and the need for ongoing monitoring and disclosure.

Deposit Mix Shift to Higher-Cost Balances

Deposit growth has leaned heavily toward interest‑bearing accounts, particularly in commercial businesses, which are materially more expensive than noninterest‑bearing funds. This shift is pressuring net interest margin even as total deposits rise, reinforcing the importance of pricing discipline and balance‑sheet management in a competitive rate environment.

Macroeconomic and Energy Price Risks

Management pointed to rising energy prices and a bifurcating consumer base as macro risks that could influence credit performance in coming quarters. Lower‑income households are showing more stress, and the bank remains cautious about how these pressures, combined with broader economic uncertainty, might affect delinquencies and losses.

NIM Outlook Uncertain

The outlook for net interest margin remains uncertain, with management expecting additional near‑term compression and emphasizing sensitivity to interest‑rate paths and deposit mix. Fewer Federal Reserve cuts than previously assumed could modestly help NII outside Markets, but overall margin trends will depend heavily on how funding costs and asset yields evolve.

Forward-Looking Guidance and Capital Plans

Looking ahead to 2026, Wells Fargo reiterated its guidance for roughly $50 billion in net interest income, including about $2 billion from Markets, and noninterest expense near $55.7 billion. The bank expects mid‑single‑digit average loan growth from late‑2025 through late‑2026, modest NIM compression but growing NII, and capital ratios held in a 10.0%–10.5% CET1 range while continuing substantial buybacks and distributions.

The earnings call painted a picture of a bank moving past legacy issues and leaning into growth, even as it manages spread pressure and elevated expenses. With strong underlying profitability, broad segment momentum, and constructive capital and regulatory dynamics, Wells Fargo’s trajectory appears positive, though investors will watch margin trends, credit quality, and NDFI exposures closely in the quarters ahead.

data.title: CarMax Earnings Call: Margin Pain, Strategy Gains
data.description:
CarMax Inc ((KMX)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 30% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

CarMax’s latest earnings call struck a cautious but constructive tone as management balanced clear operational progress against pronounced financial headwinds. Executives stressed that sales trends, cost cuts, and product initiatives are moving in the right direction, even as margins, earnings per share, and one-time charges weigh heavily on near-term results.

Sales Stabilization and Unit Growth

CarMax reported total sales of $5.9 billion, down 1% year over year, but unit volumes showed modest growth. Combined retail and wholesale vehicle sales reached about 304,000 units, up 1%, marking a notable improvement in momentum versus the weakness seen in the second and third quarters.

Improved Comparable Trends vs Prior Quarters

Used unit comparable sales fell 1.9% in the fourth quarter, still negative but markedly better than the 6.3% drop in the second quarter and 9% in the third. Management framed this as evidence that recent pricing and marketing changes are beginning to translate into better traffic and conversion.

Pricing, Marketing and Digital Actions Driving Conversion

Average selling price was $26,019, down a modest $114 per unit from a year earlier as CarMax leaned on targeted price reductions to spur demand. Leaders highlighted pricing as the biggest contributor to better trends, aided by increased acquisition marketing and early benefits from digital enhancements on the shopping journey.

Wholesale and Sourcing Strength

Wholesale unit sales rose 3% year over year, underscoring resilience in that channel despite market volatility. The company bought roughly 270,000 vehicles during the quarter and sourced about 229,000 from consumers, with about half of those acquisitions coming through its online instant appraisal platform.

SG&A Reduction Progress and Increased Target

Adjusted SG&A excluding restructuring charges came in at $577 million, down 5% from last year, signaling real traction on efficiency. In a key development, CarMax raised its fiscal 2027 exit-rate SG&A reduction target to $200 million from $150 million, with full annual run-rate benefits expected by fiscal 2028.

Capital Allocation and Share Repurchases

The company repurchased 1.3 million shares for $50 million in the quarter, taking advantage of what it sees as long-term value in the stock. Even so, with $1.31 billion of authorization still available, management has paused further buybacks to keep leverage in check, signaling a more conservative capital stance.

CAF Penetration and Funding Progress

CarMax Auto Finance originated nearly $1.9 billion in loans with penetration of 42.8%, slightly higher than a year ago, and held its weighted average contract rate at 11.1%. Net interest margin improved to 6.3%, helped by funding actions and a push into full-spectrum lending backed by diversified on- and off-balance sheet structures.

Product Enhancements and Expected Margin Upside

The redesigned extended protection plan, including MaxCare and MaxCare Plus, has now been rolled out nationally and is a clear earnings lever for coming years. Management expects this product to add roughly $35 of margin per vehicle in fiscal 2027 as adoption ramps, offering a cushion against pressure in core used vehicle margins.

Operational and Cultural Strengths

Executives emphasized ongoing work to lower the cost of bringing cars to market, including reconditioning and cost of goods sold initiatives meant to protect profitability. They also pointed to being named to Fortune’s 100 Best Companies to Work For list for the 22nd consecutive year as evidence of a durable culture supporting execution.

Net Loss and EPS Pressure

The quarter swung to a net loss of $0.85 per diluted share compared with earnings of $0.58 a year earlier, reflecting both weaker margins and sizable charges. On an adjusted basis, EPS fell to $0.34 from $0.64, a drop of about 47%, highlighting the earnings drag from a tougher operating and credit environment.

Goodwill Impairment and Restructuring Charges

Results were heavily impacted by a noncash goodwill impairment charge equal to $0.99 per share, tied to a reassessment of long-term assumptions in parts of the business. Additional restructuring charges of $0.20 per share brought the total EPS impact of these items to roughly $1.19 for the quarter.

Gross Profit and Margin Declines

Total gross profit fell 9% to $605 million as pressure mounted across major revenue streams, especially used vehicles. Used retail gross profit dropped 10% to $383 million, with profit per unit falling $207 to $2,115, while wholesale gross profit per unit slid $105 to $940, pushing wholesale margins down 7%.

Service and Other Gross Profit Pressures

Other gross profit, which includes service, came in at $107 million, down 11% year over year, reflecting softer seasonal sales and the absence of prior-year cost coverage levers. Management noted that while service faced quarterly pressure, the segment returned to profitability for the full year, giving some comfort on its trajectory.

CAF Income and Credit Provisions

CAF income declined 10% to $144 million as higher funding costs and evolving credit trends took a toll on profitability. Loan loss provisions rose to $74 million from $68 million a year ago, lifting total reserves to $453 million, or 2.78% of loans, to reflect increased expected charge-offs as lending expands further into lower credit tiers.

Occupancy and One-Time SG&A Charges

Occupancy expenses increased by $27 million, including a $21 million charge from the early abandonment of an office lease tied to prior acquisitions. These one-time costs contributed to near-term SG&A pressure but are expected to support longer-term savings as the footprint is optimized.

Guidance: Continued Margin Pressure and Efficiency Push

Looking ahead to fiscal 2027, CarMax expects used margins to decline broadly in line with the fourth quarter year-over-year trend, with the steepest pressure in the first quarter as it laps record profitability and keeps pricing aggressive. Management plans roughly $400 million in capital spending, fewer store openings, an SG&A exit-rate cut of $200 million, and a pause on buybacks while CAF pursues broader credit penetration and higher retained finance income under tight provisioning.

CarMax’s earnings call painted a picture of a retailer leaning into growth levers and cost discipline while navigating a challenging profit backdrop. For investors, the story is one of near-term margin compression but a clear roadmap around efficiency, financing, and product upsell that could reset earnings power once the used car market and credit cycle normalize.

data.description:
FB Financial Corporation ((FBK)) has held its Q1 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 30% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

FB Financial Corporation’s latest earnings call struck a cautiously upbeat tone, with management highlighting solid profitability, improving efficiency, and strong capital while acknowledging pressure on margins and fee income. Executives stressed that momentum is building through the year, particularly in lending pipelines, and framed 2026 as a year of disciplined growth rather than aggressive balance-sheet expansion.

Customer Satisfaction Crown Adds Strategic Credibility

FirstBank’s top ranking in J.D. Power’s South Central Retail Banking study gave management a marquee talking point, reinforcing its customer-first narrative. Being rated #1 for overall satisfaction, client trust, and quality of people offers independent proof that its relationship banking model is resonating in a competitive market.

Solid EPS Underscore Resilient Core Earnings

FB Financial posted GAAP EPS of $1.10 and adjusted EPS of $1.12, translating into net income of $57.5 million, or $58.3 million on an adjusted basis. These results demonstrated that the bank is generating steady bottom-line performance even as it navigates margin compression and softer noninterest revenues.

PPNR Near Target Signals Strong Core Profitability

Pretax pre-provision net revenue rose to $77.2 million, or $78.2 million adjusted, up from $71.1 million in the prior quarter. PPNR return on average assets landed around 1.93% on a reported basis and 1.95% adjusted, essentially at management’s 2% benchmark for core earning power.

Tangible Book Value Compounding Remains A Key Strength

Management underscored long-term value creation with tangible book value per share, excluding AOCI, compounding at 11.6% annually since the 2016 IPO. That growth rate signals consistent capital build and disciplined balance-sheet management, a key attraction for long-term shareholders.

Expense Discipline Drives Efficiency Improvements

Noninterest expense fell to $95.2 million, about an 11% sequential decline, or roughly 7% on an adjusted basis, reflecting tight cost control post-integration. The efficiency ratio improved to 55.2%, or 54.3% adjusted, with the banking segment at an impressive 50.9% adjusted and full-year banking expenses guided to $325–$335 million.

Capital And Liquidity Offer Strategic Flexibility

FB Financial highlighted robust capital with a CET1 ratio of 11.5%, Tier 1 leverage of 10.4%, and total risk-based capital of 13.4%. These levels give the bank room to support growth, absorb volatility, and repurchase stock opportunistically, which management did during the quarter.

Loan Pipeline Builds Momentum Into Later Quarters

While early-quarter loan growth was subdued, management flagged March as the strongest month, with upper single-digit growth that lifted confidence in the pipeline. They expect on-balance-sheet conversions to accelerate into the second and third quarters, supporting full-year mid- to high-single-digit loan and deposit growth skewed to the back half.

Acquisition Synergies On Track Post Southern States Deal

The integration of Southern States is now largely complete, with cost synergies landing essentially on plan and variances described as immaterial. Merger and integration expenses have tapered as expected, allowing the bank to fully shift focus from integration to organic growth and optimization.

New Talent And SBA Expansion Broaden Revenue Engines

FB Financial added around 15 revenue producers in the first quarter, including relationship managers and mortgage and consumer/small business lenders. The launch and expansion of an SBA lending line is intended to deepen small-business relationships and diversify fee and loan origination channels.

NIM Compression And Lower Guidance Reflect Rate Reality

Net interest margin slipped to 3.94% as balance-sheet mix and the full-quarter impact of late-year rate cuts weighed on spreads. Management trimmed full-year NIM guidance, excluding accretion, to 3.7%–3.8%, with the second quarter expected to trend toward the lower end of that range.

Noninterest Income Hit By Mortgage Volatility

Noninterest income declined by $2.4 million quarter-over-quarter, mainly due to weaker secondary mortgage volume and fewer one-off benefits recognized this time. Fewer calendar days added a small headwind, particularly to mortgage-related fees, underscoring how sensitive that line remains to both volume and timing.

Competitive Pricing Raises Funding And Loan Yield Pressure

Management pointed to intense competition from larger regionals and aggressive community banks across both loan and deposit pricing. Hot pricing in CDs and other interest-bearing products is pressuring deposit costs and loan yields, making disciplined pricing and relationship depth more critical to protecting margin.

Loan Growth Slow Start Blunted By Elevated Payoffs

Annualized loan growth of about 4% early in the year lagged internal expectations as elevated payoffs and amortization of $500–$600 million per quarter offset new production. Deposits grew roughly 5% annualized, but the elevated runoff makes achieving net balance growth more challenging, reinforcing the need for a strong pipeline.

New Deposits Carry Higher Costs Than Legacy Base

Total deposit costs edged down to 2.27%, but rates on new production were roughly 2.7%, underscoring that incremental funding is more expensive than the existing book. If the bank continues to aggressively gather deposits at these levels, aggregate funding costs could tick higher, adding further NIM pressure.

Credit Metrics Normalizing But Still Benign

Provision expense came in around $3 million, with the allowance covering 1.49% of loans held for investment, indicating continued conservative reserving. Annualized net charge-offs rose slightly to just 11 basis points, driven by a few isolated borrower situations rather than broad credit deterioration.

Seasonal And Calendar Quirks Modestly Weighed On Fees

Management noted that the quarter had two fewer calendar days than the prior period, which modestly reduced revenue and fee generation. The impact was most visible in mortgage fees, where fewer days for closings and processing slightly masked underlying activity levels.

Guidance Centers On Disciplined Growth And Margin Management

Looking ahead, FB Financial expects mid- to high-single-digit loan and deposit growth for the full year, weighted toward the latter half as March’s strong loan growth carries forward. The bank is targeting NIM of 3.7%–3.8% excluding accretion, an efficiency ratio in the low-50% range, modest credit costs, and continued capital flexibility, while remaining disciplined on pricing and opportunistic on buybacks.

FB Financial’s earnings call painted a picture of a bank leaning on strong capital, cost control, and customer loyalty to offset macro and competitive pressures. For investors, the key watchpoints will be how quickly loan growth converts from pipeline to balances, whether NIM can stabilize within guidance, and how effectively management balances growth, pricing discipline, and shareholder returns.

data.title: Th International Earnings Call Signals Profitable Reset
data.description:
Th International Limited ((THCH)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 30% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

Th International Limited’s latest earnings call struck a cautiously optimistic tone, as management highlighted solid full‑year growth and improving margins while acknowledging pressure from discounts, delivery costs, and store pruning. Executives framed 2025 as a transition year, with groundwork laid for leaner operations and more profitable expansion starting in 2026.

System Sales Growth and Network Expansion

Total system sales climbed 7.6% in 2025 to RMB 1.57 billion, underscoring the strength of the brand despite macro and competitive headwinds. The network expanded to 1,047 stores across 92 cities, with 25 net new openings during the year as management balanced growth with selective closures.

Loyalty Engine and Customer Engagement

The company’s loyalty flywheel accelerated, with registered members surging 29% year over year to more than 31 million by year‑end. Average members per store exceeded 29,600, and monthly average transacting customers in the fourth quarter jumped 14.3% to 3.43 million, pointing to deeper engagement.

Digital and Delivery as Growth Drivers

Digitalization continued to reshape the business, with digital orders rising to 89.3% of total orders in the fourth quarter from 86.1% a year earlier. Delivery orders grew 33.7% year over year in the same period, helping push Q4 system sales up 4.0% to RMB 359.4 million.

Product Innovation and Mix Optimization

Innovation remained a key revenue lever as Th International launched 178 new products in 2025, including 96 beverages and 82 food items that generated over a quarter of total sales. Non‑coffee beverages expanded their share of beverage cups to about 18.3% from 14%, while cumulative bagel and bagel sandwich sales surpassed 80 million.

Franchise Pipeline and Expansion Strategy

The franchise model is gaining traction, with more than 10,000 applications received since the individual franchise business launched in late 2023 and over 300 franchise stores opened by the end of 2025. Franchised locations increased from 446 to 485 year over year, and management is targeting at least 100 net store openings in 2026.

Corporate Margin Improvement and Efficiency

Profitability at the corporate level improved as adjusted EBITDA margin expanded by about 1 percentage point for the full year, with a 3.3‑point jump in the fourth quarter. Management credited operational efficiencies, supply‑chain optimization, and tighter cost controls for the uplift, even as revenue growth moderated.

Unit Economics of New Store Vintages

Newer company‑owned stores are delivering attractive returns, with the 2024 vintage generating nearly 15% store contribution margins in 2025. These units are expected to achieve payback in two to three years, and management anticipates similar unit economics for 2024 and 2025 openings.

Cost Efficiency Gains Across the P&L

The company reported broad‑based cost improvements, with food and packaging costs as a percentage of company‑owned store revenue falling 1.4 points to 30.1%. Store labor and other operating expenses also edged lower as a share of revenue, while adjusted G&A dropped 7.4 points, helped by lower credit losses.

Revenue Pressure and Q4 Decline

Despite full‑year growth, total revenue in the fourth quarter fell 7.3% year over year as Th International pruned underperforming company‑owned stores. Management framed these closures, especially of remote made‑to‑order express sites, as necessary to clean up the base and support healthier long‑term unit economics.

Same‑Store Sales and Discount Headwinds

Comparable transactions rose 2.7% in 2025, but system‑wide same‑store sales slipped 2.4% as heftier delivery discounts weighed on average ticket. The company cited aggressive subsidy tactics from delivery aggregators and intensifying competition from low‑priced local chains as key sources of pressure.

Store Contribution Margin Compression

Company‑owned store contribution margin narrowed to 7.0% in 2025 from 7.4% a year earlier, reflecting elevated delivery‑related costs and promotional intensity. Management characterized these pressures as temporary and tied to current aggregator dynamics rather than structural deterioration in the store model.

Liquidity and Cash Position

The balance sheet showed strain from expansion and restructuring, with cash, equivalents, time deposits, and restricted cash dropping to RMB 129.7 million from RMB 184.2 million. This decline came even after drawing on bank lines and issuing convertible notes, highlighting the capital demands of the growth plan.

Marketing Spend and Competitive Positioning

Marketing expenses rose by 1.2 percentage points as a share of revenue as the company pushed promotions to drive traffic in a crowded market. Management signaled that elevated marketing remains part of defending and growing share while newer products and channels scale.

Delivery Dependency and Margin Trade‑Offs

The strong ramp in delivery orders has become a double‑edged sword, adding volume but squeezing margins through higher fees and platform discounts. Th International is responding by negotiating with aggregators and selectively raising delivery prices in an effort to rebalance growth and profitability.

Store Pruning and Portfolio Optimization

Strategic pruning of weaker stores, especially in remote locations, weighed on near‑term revenue and shrank the company‑owned footprint. Management stressed that these cuts should enhance average unit economics, enabling more sustainable growth as future openings focus on higher‑quality sites.

Leverage and Financing Complexity

The company refinanced part of its balance sheet in December by issuing USD 89.9 million in senior secured convertible notes and amending existing instruments, which repaid variable‑rate notes due 2026. While the move extends maturities, it also underscores reliance on capital markets and adds complexity to the firm’s liquidity profile.

Guidance and Outlook for 2026

For 2026, management is prioritizing profitability and disciplined expansion, targeting at least 100 net new stores across company‑owned and franchised formats, including special channels such as railways and hospitals. They aim to cut food and packaging costs by 1–2 percentage points, mitigate delivery‑related costs via pricing and aggregator talks, sustain or improve store contribution margins above the 7% 2025 level, and further widen adjusted EBITDA margins.

Th International’s call painted a picture of a brand with strong customer traction and improving cost discipline, but also one navigating intense price competition and rising delivery dependence. Investors will watch whether the 2026 focus on unit economics, cost reduction, and targeted expansion can convert today’s cautious optimism into durable earnings growth.

While the average loan book grew, sizable early payoffs late in the quarter weighed on end‑of‑period loan balances. Management also flagged additional large payoffs expected in the second quarter, which will further obscure the underlying strength in new loan origination activity.

Albertsons Companies, Inc. ((ACI)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Albertsons’ latest earnings call struck a cautiously optimistic tone, as management showcased solid profitability, accelerating digital and loyalty trends, and a larger productivity program while openly flagging pressure from pharmacy headwinds and softer units in lower‑income shoppers. Leaders stressed that AI‑driven efficiencies and disciplined capital returns should offset near‑term drags and support modest growth into fiscal 2026.

Q4 Adjusted EBITDA and EPS Beat

Albertsons delivered Q4 adjusted EBITDA of $903 million, including roughly $68 million from the extra 53rd week, and adjusted EPS of $0.48, topping expectations despite a tough pharmacy backdrop. The beat underscored strong cost control and execution even as prescription activity and drug economics weighed on same‑store sales.

Full-Year Financial Performance

For fiscal 2025, identical sales rose 2% year over year and adjusted EBITDA reached $3.9 billion, signaling underlying resilience in a sluggish grocery environment. Management emphasized that productivity actions and disciplined pricing helped protect margins while still investing in customer value.

Shareholder Returns and Capital Allocation

The company returned more than $1.8 billion to shareholders in fiscal 2025, including about $1.5 billion of share repurchases and $322 million in dividends. It also raised the quarterly dividend 13% to $0.68 per share and refreshed a $2 billion buyback plan, reinforcing its commitment to ongoing cash returns.

Digital and Loyalty Momentum

Digital penetration surpassed 10% of sales in Q4, with digital revenue up 16% and nearly 90% of that growth coming from higher‑margin first‑party channels. Loyalty membership climbed 12% to over 51 million customers, which the company said is boosting visit frequency and basket size as offers become more personalized.

Productivity and AI Investment Acceleration

Albertsons announced a larger three‑year productivity program totaling $2.0 billion, backed by four major AI initiatives spanning digital experience, merchandising intelligence, labor, and supply chain. It rolled out its AI‑driven Gateway replenishment platform nationwide in February, aiming to cut waste, improve in‑stock levels, and free up capital.

Operational Fulfillment Strength

Store‑based fulfillment remains a key differentiator, with more than half of digital orders filled in under three hours. Management added that most delivery households now qualify for roughly 30‑minute flash delivery, the fastest‑growing digital offering and a lever to capture time‑sensitive, higher‑value baskets.

Balance Sheet and Refinancing Actions

Albertsons closed the year with net debt at 2.24 times adjusted EBITDA, leaving room to fund both investments and shareholder returns. The company also refinanced about $2.1 billion of bonds, extending maturities and improving flexibility as it navigates choppy consumer and pharmacy markets.

Pharmacy Headwinds and IRA Impact

Pharmacy performance was a major drag, knocking about 145 basis points off Q4 identical sales, with the Inflation Reduction Act accounting for roughly 105 basis points and GLP‑1 trends about 40 basis points. Management expects the IRA to continue to weigh on reported sales by around 150 basis points in fiscal 2026.

GLP-1 Growth Moderation

Growth in GLP‑1 prescriptions has cooled as payers tightened approval criteria and direct‑to‑consumer channels increased. This moderation contributed around 40 basis points of pressure on Q4 identical sales and is expected to remain a headwind into 2026, limiting pharmacy’s ability to offset other challenges.

Industry Unit Pressure and Lower-Income Cohorts

Grocery volumes stayed under pressure, especially among lower‑income customers who are cutting trips and cross‑shopping more aggressively for value. Albertsons warned that these trends, along with macro stress on budgets, will keep Q1 2026 sales below the full‑year outlook before conditions gradually improve.

Egg Deflation and Near-Term Sales Headwind

Deflation in key categories, particularly eggs, created a noticeable drag on Q4 revenue despite stable unit trends. The company expects this price deflation to continue into Q1 2026, masking some underlying volume resilience and contributing to a softer near‑term sales cadence.

Gross Margin Slightly Lower

Fourth‑quarter gross margin, excluding fuel and LIFO, came in at 27.2%, down 25 basis points from a year earlier. Management cited mix shift toward lower‑margin digital, along with pricing and mix dynamics, partially offset by productivity gains that helped keep profitability intact.

Recorded Opioid Settlement Framework Charge

Albertsons booked a $774 million charge tied to a proposed nationwide opioid settlement framework, to be paid over nine years. While non‑cash initially, the accrual represents a sizable legal and cash‑flow consideration that investors must factor into longer‑term capital allocation.

Higher Interest Expense and Extra-Week Effects

Interest expense climbed to $141 million in Q4 from $101 million a year earlier, reflecting higher borrowings and the extra 53rd week. The company also noted that adjusted EBITDA benefited by about $68 million from that additional week, an important adjustment when comparing growth rates.

Digital Mix Depressing Near-Term Margins

Rapid digital adoption is a double‑edged sword, as online orders now carry lower margins than in‑store purchases and are pressuring the overall gross margin rate. Management is leaning on its expanded productivity program and AI tools to offset these mix pressures while continuing to invest in price and convenience.

Guidance and Forward-Looking Outlook

For fiscal 2026, Albertsons guided to identical sales growth of 0% to 1%, or 1.5% to 2.5% excluding the expected IRA drag, with Q1 below that range but improving through the year. The company sees adjusted EBITDA of $3.85 billion to $3.925 billion, adjusted EPS between $2.22 and $2.32, and plans $2.0 billion to $2.2 billion of capital spending alongside a refreshed $2 billion buyback and higher dividend.

Albertsons’ call painted a picture of a retailer balancing near‑term macro and pharmacy headwinds with tangible progress in digital, loyalty, and AI‑enabled productivity. For investors, the story is one of steady, not spectacular, growth, but with disciplined leverage, rising shareholder returns, and the potential for earnings to grind higher as efficiency gains compound.

BlackRock Inc. struck an upbeat tone on its latest earnings call, underscoring double‑digit growth in revenue and operating income alongside expanding margins and strong organic fee momentum. Management acknowledged market volatility, higher expenses, and some flow headwinds, but stressed that record ETF inflows, private markets growth, and technology gains leave the firm solidly on the front foot.

Strong Top-Line and Profit Growth

BlackRock opened the quarter with first‑quarter revenue of $6.7 billion, up 27% year‑over‑year, powered by higher fees and contributions from recent acquisitions. Operating income climbed 31% to $2.7 billion and as‑adjusted EPS rose 11% to $12.53, reflecting core earnings strength despite some nonoperating headwinds.

Margin Expansion and Operating Leverage

Profitability improved as the as‑adjusted operating margin expanded 130 basis points to 44.5%, aided by scale and mix. Excluding performance fees, management noted the margin would have been 45.6%, up 180 basis points, reinforcing confidence in sustaining a 45%‑plus adjusted operating margin over time.

Strong Organic Fee Growth

The firm continued to demonstrate resilient underlying demand, posting 8% organic base fee growth in the quarter and 10% over the last twelve months. BlackRock has now delivered seven straight quarters at or above 5% organic base fee growth, underscoring the durability of its diversified fee engine.

Robust Net Inflows and ETF Leadership

Client demand translated into $130 billion of total net inflows in Q1, with iShares ETFs again leading the charge. ETF flows hit a record first‑quarter level of $132 billion, led by $41 billion into index bond ETFs and strong precision, core, and active ETF demand, helping ETF net base fees to double versus a year ago.

Private Markets and Alternatives Momentum

Alternatives remained a key growth pillar, with private markets net inflows of $9 billion driven by private credit and infrastructure strategies. The recently acquired HPS platform contributed around $230 million of base fees and $121 million of performance fees, while GIP V closed above its $25 billion target, signaling robust fundraising and deployment capacity.

Technology Services and Data Strength

BlackRock’s technology and data franchises posted a 22% year‑over‑year increase in services and subscription revenue, reflecting rising demand for its platforms. The addition of Preqin contributed about $65 million of revenue in the quarter, and management highlighted 14% annual contract value growth toward a long‑term mid‑teens ACV target.

Wealth Channel and Direct Indexing Traction

In the wealth channel, retail net inflows reached $15 billion, underpinned by record demand for Aperio direct indexing solutions. Aperio drew $13 billion of net inflows and has more than tripled its assets under management since acquisition, while options platform Spider Rock added over $1 billion, extending a nine‑quarter streak of retail inflows.

Capital Returns and Balance Sheet Actions

BlackRock continued to balance growth investments with shareholder returns, repurchasing $450 million of stock in the quarter. The company also recorded $66 million of net investment gains from equity method and minority holdings, adding to its capital flexibility as it integrates recent acquisitions.

Index and Cash Outflows

Not all flows were positive, as institutional index strategies saw $35 billion of net outflows, concentrated in low‑fee equity mandates. The firm’s cash management business also experienced $6 billion of net outflows, largely tied to seasonal redemptions from U.S. government money market funds.

Rising Expenses

Cost growth was notable, with total expenses rising 24% year‑over‑year as compensation and benefits increased 27% on higher incentives and headcount. Sales, asset, and account servicing costs climbed 25%, and G&A was up 14%, with the HPS and Preqin acquisitions materially contributing to the overall expense build.

Market Volatility and Geopolitical Uncertainty

Management flagged a challenging backdrop featuring sharp moves across rates, equities, and currencies alongside elevated geopolitical risk. They cited March as the weakest month for broad markets since late 2022, acknowledging that such conditions may create short‑term pressure even as they seek to capitalize on dislocations.

Private Credit and Retail Moderation Risk

Within private credit, executives pointed to some moderation in retail‑oriented vehicles, with softer new subscription activity and potential for higher redemptions in certain evergreen products. They attributed part of the slowdown to seasonality and uncertainty, while emphasizing that long‑term client interest in private credit remains intact.

Earnings Headwinds from Nonoperating Items and Tax

Despite robust operating trends, EPS growth of 11% lagged the pace of revenue and operating income gains, due in part to lower nonoperating income. A higher effective tax rate and a larger share count following the HPS acquisition also weighed on per‑share results, illustrating that accounting factors can mask underlying business momentum.

Concentration Risk in Certain Flows

The company acknowledged that some flow items were heavily influenced by a small number of mandates and products, adding volatility to quarterly figures. For example, institutional active inflows were partly offset by client‑specific fixed income redemptions, while subscriptions into certain flagship products were described as meaningfully variable from quarter to quarter.

Forward-Looking Guidance and Strategic Targets

Looking ahead, BlackRock reaffirmed plans to repurchase at least $450 million of shares per quarter for the rest of the year and guided to a roughly 25% tax rate. Management reiterated targets for 45%‑plus adjusted operating margins with room to trend higher, sustained low‑ to mid‑teens tech ACV growth, and organic base fee growth of 5% or more, supported by ETF and active ETF expansion.

BlackRock’s earnings call painted the picture of a firm using scale and diversification to grow through volatility while strategically investing in private markets and technology. While expense growth, market swings, and lumpy flows present ongoing challenges, the company’s record inflows, expanding margins, and clear capital‑return plans will likely keep investors focused on its long‑term earnings power.

Johnson & Johnson’s latest earnings call struck an overall optimistic tone, with management leaning on strong operational sales growth, high‑velocity oncology and immunology drugs, and a robust launch pipeline. While profitability metrics were pressured by the steep STELARA decline and heavy investment in new products, executives repeatedly framed 2026 as an investment year that sets up faster growth and margin recovery toward decade end.

Broad-Based Sales Growth Despite STELARA Drag

Johnson & Johnson reported Q1 2026 worldwide sales of $24.1 billion, translating to operational growth of 6.4% and pointing to about $100.8 billion of full‑year revenue at the midpoint of guidance. Stripping out the roughly 540‑basis‑point headwind from the sharp STELARA decline, management emphasized that underlying company growth was solidly in the double digits, led by 8.3% in the U.S. and 3.9% ex‑U.S.

Upgraded Full-Year Outlook Signals Confidence

The company raised its 2026 operational sales outlook to 5.9%–6.9%, implying 6.4% growth at the midpoint and 6.5%–7.5% on a reported basis, helped by a 53rd week. Adjusted operational EPS guidance was nudged higher by $0.02 to a range of $11.30–$11.50, with the midpoint up 5.7%, while reported adjusted EPS is now expected to land around $11.55, an increase of 7.1%.

Innovative Medicine Delivers Through Product Transitions

Innovative Medicine generated $15.4 billion in sales, up 7.4% operationally despite a daunting 920‑basis‑point drag from STELARA erosion. Management highlighted that ten brands posted double‑digit gains and that 28 platforms or products already produce at least $1 billion annually, underlining a diversified revenue base supported by fresh launches such as ICOTYDE and new subcutaneous oncology formulations.

Oncology and Immunology Stars Fuel Growth

High‑growth therapies again stole the show, with DARZALEX delivering nearly $4.0 billion in sales, up about 18%, and CARVYKTI surging 57.4% to roughly $600 million. Additional momentum came from TECVAYLI, TALVEY, RYBREVANT with LAZCLUZE, ERLEADA and the immunology workhorse TREMFYA, which grew about 63.8% and is now projected by management to ultimately surpass $10 billion in peak annual revenue.

ICOTYDE Launch Points to a New Psoriasis Era

The oral IL‑23 peptide ICOTYDE, approved for first‑line plaque psoriasis, is off to a rapid commercial start that management described as market‑expanding. With a day‑one launch, the first patient was on therapy within 24 hours, and early metrics showed about 1,500 prescriptions and more than 1,000 unique prescribers, reinforcing expectations that ICOTYDE could ultimately become a multibillion‑dollar franchise.

MedTech Grows on Electrophysiology and Structural Heart

MedTech delivered $8.6 billion in sales, representing 4.6% operational growth and led by high‑value niches rather than broad surgical strength. Electrophysiology climbed 9.5% helped by new VARIPULSE launches, while Abiomed advanced 14.4% on stronger Impella use and Shockwave grew 18.1% as intravascular lithotripsy adoption continued to expand, backed by a string of device approvals and positive data readouts.

INLEXZO Gains Traction with Key Reimbursement Win

New device INLEXZO posted just over $30 million in Q1 sales but appears poised for acceleration after securing a permanent J‑code in early April. Management noted that roughly one in five eligible patients started on INLEXZO during Q1, and after the new reimbursement took effect, new patient insertions jumped about 50% in the first week and roughly 90% in the second week.

Solid Balance Sheet, Strong Cash Generation and Dividend Lift

The company ended the quarter with about $22 billion of cash and marketable securities against $55 billion of debt, leaving net debt around $33 billion. Free cash flow was $1.5 billion in the quarter, with management reiterating expectations for roughly $21 billion for the full year, and the board approved a 3.1% dividend increase to $5.36 annually, marking the 64th consecutive year of dividend growth.

Scaling U.S. Manufacturing and R&D Investment

Johnson & Johnson underscored its long‑term commitment to domestic production and innovation through a $55 billion U.S. investment plan extending through early 2029. About $12 billion is expected to be deployed by the end of 2025, roughly 22% of the target, with substantial additional capital earmarked for manufacturing, technology and R&D projects in 2026 and beyond.

Rich Pipeline and 2026 Catalysts in Focus

Management laid out a busy 2026 pipeline agenda, citing regulatory and data milestones across oncology, immunology and neuroscience as key growth drivers. Among the highlights are additional TREMFYA indications, further ERLEADA readouts, expansion for INLEXZO, important inflammatory bowel disease and bipolar mania data, and several MedTech submissions including OTTAVA and advanced electrophysiology systems.

STELARA Decline Creates a Heavy Growth Headwind

The biggest drag on reported results came from STELARA, whose sales plunged 61.7% year over year as biosimilars, newer drug classes and an unfavorable patient mix took hold. Management quantified STELARA’s impact as about a 540‑basis‑point headwind to overall company growth and an even larger 920‑basis‑point obstacle within the Innovative Medicine segment, complicating year‑over‑year comparisons.

GAAP EPS Hit by Comparability and Special Items

GAAP net earnings fell to $5.2 billion, translating into diluted EPS of $2.14 compared with $4.54 a year earlier, a drop driven heavily by lapping prior one‑time benefits. On an adjusted basis, net earnings were $6.6 billion and EPS came in at $2.70, down 1.4% and 2.5% respectively, signaling that while core operations are growing, reported earnings still face visible pressure and volatility.

Margin Deleveraging Reflects Heavy Launch Spending

Profitability compressed across the portfolio, with Innovative Medicine’s adjusted margin slipping from 42.5% to 39.7% as launch costs and mix weighed on returns. MedTech’s margin fell from 25.9% to 22.3%, while enterprise adjusted income before tax margin declined from 36.6% to 32.5%, highlighting the near‑term trade‑off between funding growth assets and maintaining peak margin levels.

Earnings Distorted by Prior-Year One-Time Benefits

The company’s other income and expense line swung from $7.3 billion of income in the prior‑year quarter to a net expense of $294 million, largely because of a roughly $7 billion reserve reversal booked last year. Management stressed that this significant one‑off item is creating noisy comparisons and masking underlying operating performance trends when investors look at year‑on‑year EPS.

Softer Q1 Free Cash Flow but Full-Year Intact

First‑quarter free cash flow of about $1.5 billion points to a weaker run‑rate than implied by the full‑year $21 billion goal, as timing changes in U.S. rebate payments and higher domestic capital expenditures weighed on cash conversion. Executives maintained that these effects are temporary and that free cash flow should ramp over the remainder of 2026 as working capital normalizes and new launches scale.

MedTech Faces Competitive and Pricing Pressures

Not all MedTech categories kept pace, with the Surgery franchise growing only 1.2% amid a planned transformation program and rising competition in energy devices and endocutters, as well as volume‑based procurement in China. U.S. surgical vision also encountered tougher competition, causing near‑term softness even as global demand for premium intraocular lenses remained healthy.

Higher Interest Burden Weighs on Bottom Line

The interest line shifted from a net income contribution of $128 million in Q1 2025 to a net expense of $43 million in Q1 2026, as average cash balances declined and debt levels increased. This change added another layer of pressure to reported earnings and underscores how balance sheet mix can influence net income even when operating trends are positive.

Execution Risks Around Spending and China Pricing

Management acknowledged that heavier SG&A spending, which deleveraged by about 180 basis points, and stepped‑up launch investments will keep margins under pressure in the first half. They also flagged potential second‑half volume‑based procurement impacts in China for electrophysiology products, calling for disciplined execution and cost control to offset these external and self‑inflicted headwinds.

Guidance Underscores Growth Ambition and Margin Recovery

Forward‑looking guidance remains constructive, with the company raising sales expectations and slightly lifting EPS while reiterating a target for at least 50 basis points of adjusted pretax operating margin improvement in 2026. Management continues to project roughly $21 billion in free cash flow, maintain assumptions for interest and tax, and align capital priorities around funding a $55 billion U.S. investment plan and growing the dividend alongside earnings.

The earnings call painted a picture of a company in transition, absorbing the impact of a fading blockbuster and higher spending while leaning into a new generation of growth drivers. For investors, the main takeaways are resilient top‑line momentum, a deep late‑stage pipeline and clear confidence in long‑term double‑digit growth, offset by near‑term earnings noise, thinner margins and the need for flawless execution on launches and cost discipline.
